title: The return of appetite and pleasure in eating after restriction
author: Kalesh

metaDescription:
When food stops being the enemy 

 I remember the first time I tasted a strawberry after years of restriction. Not the kind of restriction that comes from...
htmlContent:
When food stops being the enemy

I remember the first time I tasted a strawberry after years of restriction. Not the kind of restriction that comes from a diet, though I'd done plenty of that too. I mean the kind that comes from your body believing food is unsafe. The kind where your nervous system has decided that pleasure itself is a threat. I bit into that strawberry and something shifted. I tasted sweetness, actual sweetness, without the voice in my head screaming warnings about sugar, calories, or losing control. I almost cried right there in my kitchen.

Here's the thing. When you've lived through complex trauma, your relationship with food isn't about willpower. It's about survival. Your body learned that pleasure is dangerous because pleasure meant vulnerability, and vulnerability meant you could get hurt. So you tightened the reins. You controlled what went in because you couldn't control what happened to you. Food became a battleground where you could win, even when everything else felt like a loss.

But here's what nobody tells you about recovery. The return of appetite, the return of pleasure in eating, it's not just about food. It's a sign that your nervous system is starting to trust the world again. It's a sign that your body believes it's safe enough to enjoy something. And that's a really big deal.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma lives in the body, not just the mind. He'd say that appetite is a biological signal that your nervous system has shifted from defense mode to connection mode. When you're stuck in fight, flight, or freeze, your digestion shuts down. Your hunger signals get scrambled. Your ability to taste and enjoy food gets muted because your body is focused on survival, not pleasure. The return of appetite means your body is coming back online. It's saying, "I'm safe enough to metabolize. I'm safe enough to receive."

That's terrifying for a lot of us. Because receiving pleasure means letting your guard down. It means trusting that you won't be punished for enjoying something. And if you grew up in an environment where pleasure was punished, where your needs were ignored or shamed, then letting yourself feel good about food can feel like walking into a trap.

So if you're reading this and your appetite hasn't returned yet, or if the thought of eating without rules makes your stomach clench, I see you. You're not broken. Your body is doing exactly what it learned to do to survive. And that survival mechanism kept you alive. But now, maybe, just maybe, you're ready for something different.

The nervous system and the dinner plate

Let me tell you about Stephen Porges and his Polyvagal Theory. Porges is the researcher who mapped out how our vagus nerve, the big nerve that runs from your brainstem down to your gut, regulates our states of safety and danger. He discovered that we have a whole branch of the nervous system dedicated to social connection and rest, which he calls the ventral vagal state. When you're in ventral vagal, your digestion works properly. You can taste food. You can feel full and satisfied. You can experience pleasure without it tipping into shame.

But when you've been through trauma, your nervous system gets stuck in what Porges calls dorsal vagal or sympathetic states. That's the freeze response or the fight/flight response. In those states, your body is saying, "Danger is here. Shut down digestion. Shut down pleasure. Conserve energy for survival." You might still eat, but it's mechanical. It's disconnected. You're not really tasting anything. You're not really present.

Think about it. Have you ever eaten an entire meal and barely noticed? You looked down at an empty plate and thought, "Did I actually eat that?" That's your nervous system running on autopilot. That's your body saying, "I can't afford to be here right now. I need to get through this as quickly and numbly as possible."

So when I talk about the return of appetite, I'm talking about your nervous system slowly, carefully, learning that it's safe to come back into your body. It's not about forcing yourself to enjoy food. It's about creating conditions where your body can relax enough to feel hunger, to taste sweetness, to know when you're full.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker's work directly addresses how trauma affects our relationship with our bodies, including our relationship with food. He talks about the inner critic that keeps us restricted, that tells us we don't deserve pleasure. Reading it helped me understand that my food rules weren't about health. They were about control, and control was about safety.

Stay with me here. Because this is where it gets uncomfortable. The return of pleasure in eating isn't just about allowing yourself to eat. It's about allowing yourself to want. It's about letting yourself have needs and letting those needs be met. And if you were raised in a household where your needs were a burden, where wanting anything was met with shame or punishment, then wanting is terrifying.

I spent years eating the same five foods because they were "safe." They didn't trigger my anxiety. They didn't make me feel out of control. But they also didn't taste like anything. I wasn't eating. I was performing eating. I was going through the motions while my body screamed for nutrients, for variety, for pleasure.

The shame spiral and the way out

When appetite does start to return, it often brings a guest with it. Shame. You take a bite of something delicious and immediately the voice kicks in: "You don't deserve this. You're going to lose control. You're going to get fat. You're weak."

That voice isn't yours. It's the voice of the trauma. It's the voice of the system that taught you that you were too much, that your needs were wrong, that pleasure was dangerous. Janina Fisher, a trauma specialist who trained under Bessel van der Kolk, calls these voices "parts" of us that formed to protect us. They're not bad. They're just young, scared, and stuck in the past.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link) by Richard Schwartz. Schwartz developed Internal Family Systems, which is basically a way of talking to the different voices inside you with curiosity instead of judgment. When the shame voice shows up around food, you can say, "I see you. I know you're trying to protect me. Thank you. But I'm okay right now. I can handle this bite."

Wild, right? That you can talk to your own shame like it's a scared child instead of an enemy. Because that's what it is. That voice that tells you you can't have pleasure? It's trying to keep you safe. It remembers a time when pleasure led to punishment. It doesn't know that the danger is over. It's stuck in the past, and it's your job to gently, repeatedly, bring it into the present.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link). It has exercises specifically for working with the inner critic and for rebuilding a relationship with your body. I used it when I was first trying to eat without rules, and it helped me track the patterns without judging them.

Notice what just happened in your body when I said "eat without rules." Did your chest tighten? Did your stomach clench? Did a voice say, "That's impossible"? That's okay. That's the part of you that's scared. And it's allowed to be scared. But you don't have to listen to it. You can hear it and still take the next bite.

Pleasure as a healing practice

Here's something I learned the hard way. Pleasure is not a reward you earn after you've suffered enough. Pleasure is a biological signal that tells your nervous system, "We are safe. We can rest. We can connect." And when you let yourself experience pleasure, even in small ways, you're literally retraining your nervous system to come out of survival mode.

Dan Siegel, the interpersonal neurobiology researcher, talks about how we can change our brain through what he calls "integration." When you have a pleasurable experience and you stay present with it, you're creating new neural pathways. You're teaching your brain that pleasure is safe. That it's okay to want. That it's okay to receive.

So start small. Don't try to eat a whole pizza if your body is used to restriction. That will freak your system out. Start with one bite of something you actually want. A piece of dark chocolate. A ripe peach. A warm croissant. And here's the hard part: stay with the experience. Don't distract yourself with your phone. Don't plan the next bite. Just taste it. Notice the texture. Notice the sweetness. Notice if any shame comes up, and if it does, just say, "I see you, shame. I'm still going to enjoy this."

A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance (paid link) by Tara Brach. She talks about how we can accept ourselves exactly as we are, including our cravings, our fears, our messy attempts at healing. That acceptance is what allows us to actually change.

I won't lie to you. The return of appetite and pleasure in eating is not a linear process. Some days you'll taste everything and feel alive. Other days you'll eat mechanically and feel nothing. Some days the shame will be so loud you'll want to go back to restriction. And that's okay. That's not failure. That's healing. Healing is messy. It's two steps forward, one step back, and sometimes a sideways stumble into the ditch.

But here's what I know. Every time you take a bite and stay present, you're telling your body, "I'm here. I'm safe. You can trust me." Every time you let yourself enjoy food without punishment, you're rewriting the story that pleasure is dangerous. And every time you choose to eat because you're hungry, not because you're following a rule, you're reclaiming a part of yourself that was stolen by trauma.

So eat the strawberry. Taste it. Let yourself want it. Let yourself have it. Your body has been waiting a long time for this permission. And it's not too late. It's never too late to come back to your appetite. It's never too late to let yourself feel pleasure again.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) - Pete Walker's guide for C-PTSD survivors
  • No Bad Parts (paid link) - Richard Schwartz's introduction to Internal Family Systems
  • The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link) - Structured exercises for trauma recovery
  • Radical Acceptance (paid link) - Tara Brach's work on accepting yourself and your experiences
